questions.question_text: Who bears ultimate responsibility for proper handling of escrow deposits in Virginia?
questions.correct_answer: The principal broker of the firm
distractors: The salesperson who drafted the contract
The office manager at the branch location
The settlement attorney handling the closing
questions.explanation: In Virginia, the principal broker is ultimately responsible for the proper handling and safekeeping of all escrow and trust funds received by the brokerage firm.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an earnest money deposit on Tuesday. The contract is ratified Thursday. By when must the broker deposit the funds?
questions.correct_answer: By end of the following Thursday
distractors: By end of the following Tuesday
Within 24 hours of receiving the check
By end of the next calendar day
questions.explanation: Virginia requires deposit by the end of the fifth business day after contract ratification. Ratification on Thursday means the fifth business day falls on the following Thursday.

questions.question_text: A broker receives a $10,000 earnest money check. Where must the funds be deposited?
questions.correct_answer: A federally insured depository in Virginia
distractors: Any FDIC-insured bank in the United States
The broker's personal savings account
A title company escrow account in any state
questions.explanation: Virginia law requires that escrow funds be deposited in a federally insured depository located in Virginia, with the account designated as an escrow or trust account.

questions.question_text: An agent receives earnest money but the contract has not yet been ratified. When does the deposit deadline begin?
questions.correct_answer: The five-day clock starts at ratification
distractors: The clock starts when the agent receives the check
The clock starts when the offer is first submitted
The clock starts at the listing agreement date
questions.explanation: In Virginia, the five-business-day deposit deadline is measured from contract ratification, not from when the agent physically receives the earnest money deposit.

questions.question_text: Which designation must appear on a Virginia brokerage escrow bank account?
questions.correct_answer: It must be labeled as escrow or trust
distractors: It must include the broker's personal name
It must reference the specific property address
It must show the client's Social Security number
questions.explanation: Virginia requires that the bank account holding escrow funds be specifically designated as an escrow or trust account so it is clearly distinguished from the broker's operating funds.

questions.question_text: A Virginia broker deposits $800 of personal funds into her escrow account to cover bank fees. Is this permitted?
questions.correct_answer: No, the maximum broker funds allowed is $500
distractors: Yes, brokers may deposit any amount for fees
Yes, up to $1,000 of personal funds is allowed
No, only the Board can authorize personal deposits
questions.explanation: Virginia allows a broker to maintain up to $500 of their own funds in an escrow account to cover service charges. The $800 deposit exceeds this limit and would constitute commingling.

questions.question_text: What is commingling in the context of Virginia escrow accounts?
questions.correct_answer: Mixing broker personal funds with client funds
distractors: Depositing earnest money in a federally insured bank
Holding deposits from multiple transactions together
Transferring funds between two escrow accounts
questions.explanation: Commingling is the prohibited practice of mixing a broker's personal or business operating funds with client escrow funds. Virginia prohibits commingling except for the allowed $500 to cover bank charges.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller both want the escrow deposit placed in an interest-bearing account. What must the broker obtain?
questions.correct_answer: Written agreement from all parties stating who gets interest
distractors: Verbal consent from the buyer only
Approval from the Virginia Real Estate Board
A court order authorizing the interest arrangement
questions.explanation: Virginia permits interest-bearing escrow accounts only when all parties provide a written agreement that specifies who will receive the interest earned on the deposited funds.

questions.question_text: A brokerage firm is dissolving. What must the principal broker do with escrow funds?
questions.correct_answer: Properly dispose of all escrow funds before dissolving
distractors: Transfer all escrow funds to the Virginia Board
Distribute funds equally among remaining agents
Convert the escrow account to a personal account
questions.explanation: When a Virginia brokerage dissolves, the principal broker must ensure proper disposition of all escrow funds, returning deposits to the rightful parties or transferring them to the appropriate successor before closing the account.

questions.question_text: A seller asks the broker to place escrow funds into a non-interest-bearing account. The buyer wants interest. What should the broker do?
questions.correct_answer: Use a non-interest-bearing account since all parties must agree
distractors: Follow the buyer's preference as depositor of funds
Follow the seller's preference as property owner
Split the deposit between two separate accounts
questions.explanation: An interest-bearing escrow account requires written agreement from all parties. Since the seller does not agree, the broker must use a standard non-interest-bearing account.

questions.question_text: How long must a Virginia broker retain escrow account records?
distractors: Three years
Seven years
Ten years
questions.explanation: Virginia requires brokers to retain all escrow and trust account records for a minimum of five years. These records must be available for inspection by the Real Estate Board.

questions.question_text: The Virginia Real Estate Board notifies a broker of an upcoming escrow audit. Must the broker have advance notice?
questions.correct_answer: No, the Board may audit at any time
distractors: Yes, the Board must give 30 days written notice
Yes, the Board must obtain a court order first
No, but only during annual renewal periods
questions.explanation: The Virginia Real Estate Board has authority to audit escrow and trust accounts at any time without advance notice. Brokers must maintain records in a condition ready for inspection.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller dispute who is entitled to a $15,000 earnest money deposit. What legal action can the broker take?
questions.correct_answer: File an interpleader action with the court
distractors: Return the full amount to the buyer immediately
Split the deposit equally between both parties
Hold the funds indefinitely until they agree
questions.explanation: When parties dispute ownership of escrow funds, the Virginia broker may file an interpleader action, asking the court to hold the funds and determine the rightful recipient.

questions.question_text: During an audit, the Board finds that a broker's escrow records are four years old but incomplete. What is the likely consequence?
questions.correct_answer: Disciplinary action for inadequate record keeping
distractors: No violation since records are under five years old
A warning letter with no further consequences
Automatic license revocation by the Board
questions.explanation: Virginia requires brokers to maintain complete escrow records for five years. Incomplete records within the retention period constitute a violation subject to Board disciplinary action.

questions.question_text: A broker filed an interpleader for disputed escrow funds. Who decides how the funds are distributed?
questions.correct_answer: The court determines the rightful recipient
distractors: The principal broker makes the final decision
The Virginia Real Estate Board holds a hearing
The listing agent decides based on contract terms
questions.explanation: In an interpleader action, the broker deposits the disputed funds with the court, which then determines the rightful recipient. This relieves the broker from liability for choosing the wrong party.
